comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes neural sequence models for Japanese predicate-argument structure (PAS) analysis that explicitly model multi-predicate interactions. The authors extend a stacked bidirectional RNN single-sequence model to a multi-sequence model using Grid-RNNs and incorporate residual connections to facilitate training of deep models. Experiments on the NAIST Text Corpus 1.5 show consistent improvements over both pointwise and state-of-the-art joint baselines (Ouchi et al., 2015), especially for zero anaphora (intra-sentential Zero arguments) and the dative case.

Strengths:
- Tackles an important problem in Japanese PAS, namely modeling multi-predicate interactions without relying on syntactic features, which helps avoid parser error propagation.
- Introduces a reasonable architectural extension (Grid-RNN over predicates × tokens) and uses residual connections to enable deeper models; empirical gains are shown.
- Experimental results on a standard benchmark (NAIST 1.5) indicate clear improvements, particularly for challenging zero anaphora cases.
- The paper situates itself well relative to prior work (both traditional joint models and recent neural approaches) and provides ablations on depth and residual connections.

Weaknesses / Points needing clarification:
- The provided text has many duplicated and garbled paragraphs, which harms readability; the final paper needs careful proofreading and clean organization.
- The Grid-RNN description is high-level; the precise connectivity across predicate sequences and tokens (order of predicates, parameter sharing, boundary conditions) is not fully clear from the excerpt. More detailed architecture diagrams or pseudo-code would help reproducibility.
- Computational cost and scalability are not discussed. Modeling M sequences (one per predicate) with Grid-RNNs can be expensive for sentences with many predicates; please report training/inference time, memory use, and whether any approximations/pruning are used.
- While the paper compares to Ouchi et al. (2015) and a pointwise baseline, comparison to the recent neural methods for Japanese PAS (noting dataset issues) is described only qualitatively. A clearer discussion or reimplementation comparison would strengthen claims about state-of-the-art.
- Little error analysis is given in the excerpt: where exactly does the multi-sequence/Grid model help (types of predicates, distances, predicate interactions)? Some qualitative examples and failure cases would be valuable.
- The experiments focus on intra-sentential arguments (Dep and Zero) only. The limitation is acknowledged, but it would be helpful to discuss how the approach might be extended to inter-sentential cases or integrated with document-level models.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Clean up the manuscript to remove repeated text and improve flow. Add a clear figure showing the Grid-RNN connections across predicate and token dimensions.
- Provide implementation details: hyperparameters, embedding dimensions, number of predicates per sentence distribution, training time, GPU used, and whether any regularization/dropout was applied beyond L2.
- Include runtime and memory cost analysis; discuss practical limits and any strategies to handle sentences with many predicates.
- Add ablations that isolate the contributions of modeling predicate–predicate interactions vs. increased model capacity (e.g., control for parameter count), and perhaps experiments on smaller/deeper single-sequence baselines to rule out capacity effects.
- Present qualitative examples showing how multi-predicate interactions enable correct zero argument resolution, and include an error analysis to guide future work.

Overall assessment:
The paper presents a solid and practically useful extension of neural sequence models to jointly model multiple predicates for Japanese PAS. The empirical gains on a standard corpus are convincing, though the paper would benefit from clearer exposition, more architectural detail, runtime analysis, and deeper error analysis. Given the importance of zero anaphora in pro-drop languages and the demonstrated improvements, I recommend acceptance conditional on addressing clarity and additional experimental/analysis questions in the final version.
